title: STL: Maps & Sets
description: Master the ordered and unordered associative containers — map, set, unordered_map, unordered_set, multimap, multiset. These are the workhorses of Codeforces problems at the 1200–1800 range. You will learn when to use each, the exact complexity of every operation, and the idiomatic CP patterns that appear in contest after contest.

sections.content: A `map<K,V>` stores **key-value pairs sorted by key** using a self-balancing BST (red-black tree). Every operation — insert, lookup, erase — is **O(log n)**. In CP, maps are used for frequency counting, memoization, and coordinate compression. The key must have `operator<` defined (all built-in types do).

sections.title: map — Complete CP Reference

sections.label: The Subscript Operator Trap
sections.content: Using `map[key]` to **check** a key silently inserts it with a default value (0 for int, empty string for string). Always use `.count(key)` or `.find(key)` to check existence without side effects. This bug causes WA in many contest submissions.

sections.label: lower_bound on map
sections.content: `map.lower_bound(k)` returns an iterator to the first key >= k in O(log n). This is the clean way to answer 'find the smallest key that is at least x' — a pattern that appears in problems involving coordinate compression and offline queries.

A `set<T>` stores **unique elements in sorted order**, again backed by a red-black tree. Think of it as a `map` with no values — all the same O(log n) guarantees apply. The killer feature in CP: `lower_bound` and `upper_bound` on a set for nearest-neighbor queries.

sections.title: set — All Operations

sections.content: When you need **O(1) average** insert/lookup instead of O(log n), switch to `unordered_map` or `unordered_set`. The trade-offs: no ordering, worst-case O(n) if hash collisions occur, and they cannot use `lower_bound`. Use them for frequency tables and visited sets where order doesn't matter.

sections.title: unordered_map — Frequency Counting Pattern

sections.label: Anti-Hash Attacks on Codeforces
sections.content: Codeforces problemsetters sometimes craft test cases that cause maximum hash collisions in `unordered_map`, turning O(n) into O(n²) and causing TLE. The fix: use a custom hash — `unordered_map<int,int,custom_hash>` where custom_hash uses a random splitmix64 seed. At 1600+ rating, this becomes necessary knowledge.

A `multiset` allows duplicate values; a `multimap` allows duplicate keys. The critical CP use case for `multiset` is maintaining a **sorted bag of elements** where you can efficiently find/remove any single occurrence. The `erase(value)` call removes **all** copies — use `erase(find(value))` to remove just one.

sections.title: multiset — Sorted Bag Pattern

sections.content: These patterns are templates you should memorize. They appear in disguised forms in hundreds of Codeforces problems from Div. 2 B to D.

sections.title: Pattern 1 — Coordinate Compression
Pattern 2 — Group by Key (Anagram / Frequency Signature)
Pattern 3 — Count Distinct in Sliding Window

sections.label: map vs unordered_map Decision Guide
sections.content: Use **`map`** when: you need sorted iteration, lower/upper bound queries, or keys are pairs/custom types. Use **`unordered_map`** when: you only need lookup/insert/count and n > 10^5 (log n factor matters). When in doubt with n ≤ 10^5, `map` is fine — the constant factor of log(10^5) ≈ 17 is small.

sections.title: Exercise 1: map Subscript Behavior
sections.explanation: Initially m has 2 keys. `m["c"]` INSERTS key "c" with default value 0 and returns 0 — so size becomes 3. `m["a"]++` changes 1 to 2. `m.count("d")` checks existence WITHOUT inserting (returns 0). Size stays 3. This is the critical subscript-vs-count distinction.

sections.title: Exercise 2: set lower_bound Navigation
sections.explanation: `lower_bound(25)` returns iterator to first element >= 25, which is 30. `--it` moves to 20. `upper_bound(30)` returns iterator to first element > 30, which is 40. `*s.rbegin()` is the last (largest) element: 50.

sections.title: Exercise 3: multiset Erase Trap
sections.explanation: Initially 3 copies of 4. `erase(find(4))` erases just ONE occurrence → 2 copies remain. `erase(4)` erases ALL copies of value 4 → 0 remain. Remaining elements are {2, 6}, so size is 2.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Codeforces-Style — Most Frequent Element in Range
sections.approach: For each query, build a frequency map over the subarray in O(N). Track the best (max_count, min_value) pair as you iterate. Use a `map<int,int>` so iteration is sorted — the first key with the max count is automatically the smallest.
sections.explanation: Since `map` iterates in ascending key order, when two keys have equal count, the one encountered first (smaller key) becomes `best_val` and is never replaced (strict `>`). This elegantly handles the tie-breaking rule without extra logic.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Find the Bug — Erasing During Iteration
sections.error: Undefined behavior: after `m.erase(it)`, the iterator `it` is invalidated. Incrementing an invalidated iterator in the for-loop causes undefined behavior (crash or corruption).
sections.explanation: The STL `erase()` on associative containers returns an iterator to the element **after** the erased one. Capturing this return value and using it as the new `it` is the canonical safe pattern. Alternatively, use `std::erase_if(m, predicate)` in C++20.

sections.label: When n ≤ 10^6 matters
sections.content: For n = 10^6 with `map`: 10^6 × log(10^6) ≈ 2×10^7 operations — still fast enough. For `unordered_map` without a custom hash at this scale, collision risk increases. Profile if you get unexpected TLE, and consider the custom hash or `__gnu_pbds::gp_hash_table` as a drop-in faster alternative.
